content.problemStatement:
You are given a grid with size NĂ—MN \times MNĂ—M. Each cell of this grid contains either 000 or 111.

We should perform the following operation until the size of the grid becomes 1Ă—11 \times 11Ă—1:


Suppose the present size of the grid is xĂ—yx \times yxĂ—y.
Choose one of the xâˆ’1x-1xâˆ’1 horizontal lines or yâˆ’1y-1yâˆ’1 vertical lines crossing the grid.
Fold the grid along this line. For each pair of cells which overlap after this folding, replace them by a single cell such that the value in this cell is the XOR of the values in the two overlapping cells. Thus, we obtain a new grid.
**Example:** If the initial grid is

`000
111
010
`
After folding along 111-st horizontal line, the grid is
`111
010
`
After folding along the 222-nd vertical line, the grid is
`10
01
`
After folding along the 111-st horizontal line, the grid is
`11
`
After folding along the 111-st vertical line, the grid is
`0
`Determine whether there is a sequence of folds such that in the final 1Ă—11 \times 11Ă—1 grid, the only cell contains 111.
constraints: each character of each string is '0' or '1'
the sum of Nâ‹…MN \cdot MNâ‹…M over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM.
NNN lines follow. For each valid iii, the iii-th of these lines contains a single binary string SiS_iSiâ€‹ with length MMM describing the iii-th row of the grid.
content.outputFormat:
Print a single line containing the string `"YES"` if it is possible to obtain 111 in the final 1Ă—11 \times 11Ă—1 grid or `"NO"` if it is impossible (without quotes).

You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: There is only 111 possible move, which is to fold along the only vertical line. The resulting grid contains only 111.
Example case 2: There is no sequence of moves which would result in a 1Ă—11 \times 11Ă—1 grid containing only 111.
